coredata.dc:title: Achieving efficient phosphorescence of carbon dots via a synergistic enhancement strategy for bright field anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Jing
Yang, Daiqi
Lu, Chunyi
Li, Yi
Gu, Xingyu
Sha, Peng
Qu, Songnan
Wang, Xin
Fu, Lipei
Li, Qijun
coredata.dc:description: Phosphorescent materials that exhibit high efficiency and intensity are crucial for practical applications. In this study, we devised a novel strategy to enhance carbon dots (CDs) phosphorescence based on a crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) and layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) synergistic enhancement design. The seed CDs are initially functionalized with cationic polymers on their surfaces, followed by assembly with negatively charged matrices to enhance their phosphorescence. This leads to a significant phosphorescence intensity enhancement by a factor of more than 88, enabling visual readability even in bright fields. Their lifetime is prolonged from 0.13 to 0.41 s and the maximum phosphorescence quantum yield reached up to 27.2 %. Detailed investigations revealed that CEE and LBL significantly inhibited molecular vibrations and protected the triplet excitons of CDs from oxygen quenching, enhancing phosphorescence emission. The loading capacity of the CDs in the matrix was significantly enhanced owing to the uniform dispersion induced by the electrostatic interactions between CDs and the substrate. Furthermore, we envision potential applications of these CDs for anti-counterfeiting and crack detection in bright fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphorescence
Crosslink-enhanced emission
Layer-by-layer self-assembly
Bright field

The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Ning from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Ning from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Preparation process of PEI-CDs and PVA-PEI-CDs. b–d) TEM images, e) FTIR spectra, and f, g) XPS spectra of the seed CDs, PEI-CDs, and PVA-PEI-CDs.

a) Photographs of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper taken within 0.1 to 7 s after 395 nm light irradiation. b–d) Phosphorescence spectra of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper under different excitations. e, f) Phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper under 395 nm excitation. g) EPR spectra of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper. h) Phosphorescence intensity of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper in air and nitrogen atmospheres.

a) Photographs of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper under sunlight after 395 nm light irradiation. b) Brightness values of a mobile phone screen and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper under 395 nm UV light irradiation. c) Phosphorescence spectra of seed-CDs@PVA and PEI-CDs@PVA under different CDs loadings. d) Comparison of the aggregation degree of seed-CDs@PVA and PEI-CDs@PVA.
Possible phosphorescence emission processes of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper.

a) Cotton thread soaked in a PVA-PEI-CDs solution was weave into various patterns. b) A QR code printed by PVA-PEI-CDs applied to anti-counterfeiting encryption in a bright field. c) PVA-PEI-CDs embedded in epoxy resin for crack detection.

head.ce:title: Achieving efficient phosphorescence of carbon dots via a synergistic enhancement strategy for bright field anti-counterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy to enhance carbon dots (CDs) phosphorescence based on a crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) and layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) synergistic enhancement design. CEE and LBL significantly inhibited the molecular vibration, protected the triplet excitons of CDs from oxygen quenching, significantly enhanced the loading capacity in the matrix, and enhanced the phosphorescence emission.

Phosphorescent materials that exhibit high efficiency and intensity are crucial for practical applications. In this study, we devised a novel strategy to enhance carbon dots (CDs) phosphorescence based on a crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) and layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) synergistic enhancement design. The seed CDs are initially functionalized with cationic polymers on their surfaces, followed by assembly with negatively charged matrices to enhance their phosphorescence. This leads to a significant phosphorescence intensity enhancement by a factor of more than 88, enabling visual readability even in bright fields. Their lifetime is prolonged from 0.13 to 0.41 s and the maximum phosphorescence quantum yield reached up to 27.2 %. Detailed investigations revealed that CEE and LBL significantly inhibited molecular vibrations and protected the triplet excitons of CDs from oxygen quenching, enhancing phosphorescence emission. The loading capacity of the CDs in the matrix was significantly enhanced owing to the uniform dispersion induced by the electrostatic interactions between CDs and the substrate. Furthermore, we envision potential applications of these CDs for anti-counterfeiting and crack detection in bright fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of big data, the demand for information materials containing large amounts of data with high security has become increasingly urgent [1–3]. Phosphorescent materials with long-lived excited states show advantages in terms of time-scale security over their traditional fluorescent counterparts [4–9], thus exhibiting immense potential for advanced security applications [10,11]. The long lifetime endows phosphorescent materials with “time-dimensional” encryption capabilities—encrypted information can remain visible after the excitation light is turned off, and multi-level dynamic encryption can be achieved by modulating the phosphorescence lifetime or afterglow color. Furthermore, the combination of phosphorescent materials with time-resolved imaging technology can effectively eliminate background interference from short-lived fluorescence, further enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio and anti-tampering properties of anti-counterfeiting signals. Various anti-counterfeiting patterns and labels utilising phosphorescent materials have been developed to effectively curb the prevalence of counterfeit products [12–15]. However, most phosphorescence patterns are only visually readable in darkened environments and are unavailable in bright fields, which restricts their application in various scenarios [16–18]. Therefore, phosphorescent materials with high efficiency and brightness that are capable of emitting sufficiently bright light to ensure visual readability in bright fields are desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an important class of phosphorescent nanomaterials that has attracted considerable interest in recent years. Numerous efforts have been devoted to achieving a high phosphorescence efficiency [19–22]. To overcome spin-forbidden transitions, specific atoms with isolated electrons (e.g., N, O, and P) have been introduced into CDs, which have considerably facilitated the transition from singlet to triplet excited states [23,24]. For the stability of triplet excitons, some matrices have been used to provide a rigid environment to reduce the nonradiative relaxation and quenching processes of triplet excitons. To date, researchers have predominantly concentrated their efforts on the selection of dopant atoms and matrix types, while neglecting the interactions between CDs and the matrix [25–30]. Most CDs possess a negative charge, which generates electrostatic repulsion with commonly used matrix materials such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), fibre paper, and inorganic salts [31,32]. These repulsive forces impede the closeness between the CDs and matrices, which is detrimental to the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between them, leading to a low phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY). Furthermore, most reported CD materials exhibit weak intensities owing to their low loading capacities [33]. Increasing the concentration of CDs within the matrix enhances the luminescence intensity to a certain degree. However, at elevated concentrations, quenching occurs owing to aggregation [34]. Thus, the exploration of novel fabrication strategies to achieve high efficiency and strong phosphorescence is highly desirable.

Layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) is a highly effective strategy for preparing ultrathin multilayer films [35–39]. It uses electrostatic interactions to deposit two types of materials with positive and negative charges on the membrane surface to form a rigid active layer. If this technology is employed to construct a three-dimensional confined structure for CDs, it may significantly reinforce the rigidity of the CDs system, thus enhancing phosphorescence. Moreover, crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) proposed by Yang et al. has been an effective strategy for enhancing luminescence by suppressing the vibration and rotation of chromophores [40–44].

With this in mind, a novel strategy for the preparation of strongly phosphorescent CDs materials based on the LBL and CEE synergistic enhancement design was developed in this paper. The seed CDs were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin and then functionalized with the cationic polymer polyethyleneimine (PEI) [45–47]. Highly cross-linked domains restrict the vibration and rotation of CDs. Accordingly, the lifetime phosphorescence of CDs-inked paper is prolonged from 0.13 to 0.36 s and their phosphorescence intensity increased by a factor of 12. The cationic polymer-functionalized CDs were subsequently embedded into an aqueous solution of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The assembly between the positively charged CDs and PVA, similar to an LBL, further inhibited the nonradiative transition of the triplet excitons of the CDs. Owing to the uniform dispersion induced by electrostatic interactions, the loading capacity of CDs in the matrices was significantly enhanced. Ultimately, under the synergistic effect of CEE and LBL, the phosphorescence intensity of the CDs-inked paper increased by 88 times, making it visible in daylight, and the lifetime and efficiency of CDs-inked paper reach 0.41 s and 27.2 %, respectively. Based on their long lifetimes and highly efficient phosphorescence, we envision the potential applications of these CDs for anti-counterfeiting and crack detection in bright fields.

A schematic of the proposed CEE and LBL synergistic enhancement strategy is shown in Fig. 1 a. The seed CDs were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin at 200 °C for 3 h [17]. Subsequently, we performed surface modification using PEI at 200 °C for 3 h, resulting in the formation of PEI-CDs. Then, the PEI-CDs were mixed with PVA to construct the LBL-assembled PVA-PEI-CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterise the morphology and structure of the seed CDs, PEI-CDs, and PVA-PEI-CDs. The seed CDs possessed a uniform size dispersion with an average size of 4.6 nm (Fig. 1b). Compared with the seed CDs, the average size of the PEI-CDs increased to 6.2 nm (Fig. 1c), implying that the PEI molecules were functionalized onto the surface of the CDs. The TEM image (Fig. 1d) of the PVA-PEI-CDs shows that the PEI-CDs were homogeneously dispersed in PVA without apparent aggregation. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig. S1a) images shows that the seed-CDs have the lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the basal spacing of graphite. The X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. S1b) patterns exhibited a broad peak centered at 21.9°, which is assigned to the partial order in the interior of CDs resulting from covalently crosslinked frameworks [48].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to investigate the chemical structures of the three CDs. As shown in Fig. 1e, the absorption bands at 3400 and 1720 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of the seed CDs correspond to O–H and CO bonds, respectively. The absorption band at 1300–1500 cm−1 is ascribed to the nitrogen heterocycle, which was inherited from the levofloxacin precursor. In the FTIR spectrum of PEI-CDs, there are tensile vibrations of N–H (3423 cm−1) and amide CO groups (1631 cm−1), indicating that PEI molecules functionalized on the surfaces of seed CDs [47]. The enhanced vibrations of O–H (3220 cm−1) and C–H (2940 cm−1) in the FTIR spectrum of PVA-PEI-CDs indicate that PVA molecules were successfully adsorbed onto the surface of PEI-CDs. The FTIR results are further supported by the XPS results. As shown in Fig. 1f, the XPS spectra showed that the three CDs were mainly composed of C, O, and N. Compared with the seed CDs, the C and N contents in the PEI-CDs increased significantly. The C content in PVA-PEI-CDs was also significantly increased compared with those in PEI-CDs, indicating that PEI-CDs were coated with PVA. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of the seed CDs confirmed the presence of O–CO (287.4 eV), C–O/C–N (285.8 eV), and CC/C–C bonds (284.4 eV) (Fig. 1g). The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum of the seed CDs confirmed the presence of OC–O (532.2 eV), C–O (531.6 eV), and CO bonds (529.7 eV) (Fig. S2a). The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of the seed CDs confirm the presence of pyrrole N (399.3 eV) and C–N–C (398.1 eV) (Fig. S2d). For PEI-CDs, the O–CO bond disappeared completely, meanwhile an amide bond appeared (Fig. S2b and S2e). These results indicated that the carboxyl group on the surface of the seed CDs reacted with the amino groups in PEI to produce amide bonds. Simultaneously, the peaks of C–H/C–C and C–O/O–H in the XPS spectrum of PVA-PEI-CDs increased significantly, indicating that a large amount of PVA was incorporated (Tables S1–S3).

To facilitate their luminescence performance comparison, the three CDs were printed on paper and dried at 60 °C to obtain seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper. As shown in Fig. 2 a, phosphorescence images of seed-CDs@paper, PEI-CDs@paper, and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper were obtained under identical conditions to facilitate comparison. Seed-CDs@paper exhibited a time-dependent phosphorescence colour change from orange to green 1 s after turning off the 395 nm lamp, which was studied in our previous work [17]. The phosphorescence spectrum (Fig. 2b) of seed-CDs@paper revealed excitation-dependent phosphorescence due to dual phosphorescence emission centres. After cationic functionalization with PEI, PEI-CDs@paper exhibited long-lived yellow-green phosphorescence for 4 s after the cessation of 395 nm light excitation. The phosphorescence emission spectrum of PEI-CDs@paper (Fig. 2c) are less dependent on the excitation wavelength, with an emission peak at 575 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 395 nm, which is primarily attributed to a uniform surface state induced by the cationic polymer crosslinked on the CDs surface. The phosphorescence emission spectrum of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper (Fig. 2d) is similar to that of PEI-CDs@Paper. Compared to the seed-CDs@paper, the phosphorescence wavelength of PEI-CDs@paper demonstrates a blue shift, which is attributed to the changes in surface states and electronic properties induced by the PEI. Due to the CEE, the phosphorescence intensity of PEI-CDs@paper increased by 1200 %, and correspondingly, its average lifetime increased from 0.13 s (seed-CDs@paper) to 0.36 s (Fig. 2e, f and Table S4). Compared with seed-CDs@paper and PEI-CDs@ paper, the phosphorescence intensity of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper was further enhanced, and its average lifetime increased to 0.41 s (Table S4). The phosphorescence intensity of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper was almost 88 times that of seed-CDs@paper (Fig. 2e). Moreover, the fluorescence of PEI-CDs@paper and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper were also investigated. The results exhibited a slight enhancement compared to that of seed-CDs@paper. (Fig. S3). The yellow-green phosphorescence of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper lasted for over 7 s at room temperature (Fig. 2a). By reference to the reported method [49], we measured and calculated the QY of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper, and PQY of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper was 27.2 % (Fig. S4).

The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) pattern of seed-CDs@paper showed higher peaks at lower magnetic fields than those of PEI-CDs@paper and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper (Fig. 2g), suggesting that CEE and LBL may hinder electron transfer between triplet excitons and triplet oxygen in PEI-CDs@paper and PVA-PEI-CDs@paper. The phosphorescence intensity of seed-CDs@paper and PEI-CDs@paper under a nitrogen atmosphere is nearly 3.3 and 1.8 times higher than those under an air atmosphere, respectively, while the phosphorescence intensity of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper exhibit almost no change under different atmospheres (Figs. 2h and S5). These results indicate that CEE and LBL can significantly inhibit molecular vibrations and protect the triplet excitons of CDs from oxygen quenching, thus enhancing the phosphorescence emission.

Surprisingly, PVA-PEI-CDs@paper not only exhibited high phosphorescence efficiency but also extraordinarily strong intensity. Remarkably, after switching off the 395 nm irradiation source, the intense green-yellow phosphorescence from PVA-PEI-CDs@paper illuminated items for more than 3 s in a bright field (Fig. 3 a). The phosphoresce luminance of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper reached 500 lx at a delay of 0.1 s, which is higher than the brightness of commercial office lighting (320–500 lx), enabling them to be visible in the bright field (Fig. 3b). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of phosphorescence visibility in a bright indoor lighting environment. These characteristics endow these materials with significant potential for advanced optical anti-counterfeiting and storage applications in bright environments.

This excellent performance can be primarily attributed to the homogeneous dispersion of PEI-CDs in the negatively charged substrate through strong electrostatic interactions, effectively preventing their aggregation at high concentrations. To verify this hypothesis, we performed a control experiment. As shown in Fig. 3d, the seed CDs and PEI-CDs aqueous solutions with a certain concentration were selected for comparative analysis by dispersing them into PVA films, named seed-CDs@PVA and PEI-CDs@PVA, respectively. The daylight image clearly reveals that the PEI-CDs can be uniformly dispersed in the PVA film without apparent aggregation. The uniform luminescence (fluorescence and phosphorescence) images of PEI-CDs@PVA also indicated the homogeneous dispersion of PEI-CDs in the PVA film. In contrast, the seed CDs were severely agglomerated in the PVA film in a bright field, and the fluorescence and phosphorescence from the seed CDs were non-uniform. The phosphorescence intensities of seed-CDs@PVA and PEI-CDs@PVA with different CDs loadings are also compared in Fig. 3c. The phosphorescence emission intensity of PEI-CDs@PVA gradually increased with increasing PEI-CDs loading, reaching a maximum value at a concentration of 3.25 mg mL−1. However, for CDs@PVA, the strongest phosphorescent emission was obtained at a lower concentration of 1.63 mg mL−1, which is much lower than that of PEI-CDs@PVA, implying a low loading capacity of the seed CDs in seed-CDs@PVA. Therefore, we concluded that the seed CDs are more prone to aggregate on or among the PVA molecules, resulting in weak intensity owing to the low loading. For PEI-CDs@PVA, the loading capacity of the PEI-CDs in the matrix was significantly enhanced owing to the uniform dispersion induced by the electrostatic interactions between the PEI-CDs and PVA. Thus, PEI-CDs@paper can maintain high optical quality, even under high-loading conditions.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of PVA-PEI-CDs were measured (Fig. S6), and two absorption peaks at 290 and 332 nm attributed to the π–π* transition of CC and the n–π* transition of the N, O heterocyclic structure were detected. In the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of PVA-PEI-CDs under 575 nm emission (Fig. S6), the excitation bands located in the range from 320 to 400 nm is highly overlapped with the absorption band of the heterocycles, suggesting that the phosphorescence mainly originates from the heterocycles of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper [17]. The temperature-dependent phosphorescence spectra of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper were recorded to investigate the dynamic process of carriers. PVA-PEI-CDs@paper exhibited a gradual increase in the intensities of the phosphorescence peak at 575 nm with a decrease in temperature from 298 to 77 K (Fig. S7), which indicates their remarkable phosphorescence property.

Based on the above results, we propose a CEE and LBL synergistic enhancement strategy for the preparation of highly efficient and strong phosphorescence emissions, as shown in Fig. 4 . During the hydrothermal treatment process, the levofloxacin precursor underwent self-polymerisation and carbonisation, consequently producing the seed CDs that contained abundant nitrogen heterocyclic and CO groups on their surfaces and edges. The N- and CO-related groups have a small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, facilitating effective phosphorescence emission [17]. The seed CDs were first functionalized with cationic polymers to produce a cross-linked polymer structure on their surfaces. This cross-linked structure can significantly inhibit the vibration and rotation of the luminescence centres. Moreover, PEI-CDs with positive charges can uniformly and firmly adsorb onto the negatively charged substrate though strong electrostatic interactions, similar to the LBL effect, not only further suppressing nonradiative losses, but also avoiding their aggregation and increasing their loading content. These synergistic effects of CEE and LBL greatly enhanced the phosphorescence of the CDs, resulting in visible phosphorescence under a bright field.

The stability of the PVA-PEI-CDs@paper is an important factor for practical applications. Fig. S8 shows the cycle stability of PVA-PEI-CDs@paper under alternate excitation. No obvious intensity decreases were observed, indicating sustained signal stability. The phosphorescent intensity of the PVA-PEI-CDs@paper remains above 80 % of its initial value, when placed in an ambient environment (relative humidity of 55 %) for more than 14 days (Fig. S9a), which indicates its good stability. Furthermore, the phosphorescence intensity of the PVA-PEI-CDs@paper at different temperatures have also been studied; the results showed that as the temperature increases, the phosphorescence decreases, which is a fundamental characteristic of phosphorescence. (Fig. S9b). The prepared PVA-PEI-CDs solution can be applied not only to paper surfaces but also to various substrates, such as cotton fabric, nylon, and plastic products. As shown in Fig. S10, the PVA-PEI-CDs@cotton-fabric, PVA-PEI-CDs@nylon, and PVA-PEI-CDs@plastic show excitation-dependent phosphorescence behavior under different excitations. Their average phosphorescence lifetimes are 0.43, 0.29 and 0.41 s, respectively (Fig. S10d and Table S5). The differences of their phosphorescence peaks and average lifetimes may be caused by the interaction between CDs and the matrix. In Fig. 5 a, cotton yarns are drawn through a coating tank filled with the PVA-PEI-CDs solution, resulting in functional cotton yarns with phosphorescence emission properties that can be woven into various patterns for display applications. Similarly, anti-counterfeiting QR codes were produced using printing technologies (Fig. 5b). After exposure to 395 nm UV light, the QR code patterns displayed yellow-green phosphorescence, which could be detected in the bright field. This characteristic enhances their convenience for application to information anti-counterfeiting.

Furthermore, we explored a facile method for detecting cracks at different depths using the phosphorescent PVA-PEI-CDs based on its moisture-sensitive features. The phosphorescent layers were embedded within the epoxy resin layer at varying depths. When shallow cracks form, moisture penetrates the surface phosphorescent layer, resulting in a decrease in phosphorescence intensity. As deeper cracks developed, the phosphorescence intensity gradually decreased, ultimately leading to phosphorescence quenching. Using this method, cracks could be effectively detected at different depths. As shown in Fig. 5c, three layers of phosphorescent PVA-PEI-CDs were embedded within the epoxy resin at depths of 1, 2, and 3 mm. Cracks were created at different depths by artificial drilling. As predicted, after UV turn-off, the phosphorescence of samples with a crack depth of 2 mm was significantly reduced compared to that of samples with a crack depth of 1 mm, while samples with a crack depth of 3 mm produced almost no phosphorescence (Fig. S11).

In summary, we exploited a synergistic phosphorescence enhancement strategy for the preparation of CDs with visible phosphorescence under a bright field. The CEE and LBL synergistic enhancement design considerably suppressed nonradiative losses and improved the loading capacity of CDs, thus leading to a highly efficient and bright phosphorescence. Significantly, the CDs materials exhibited a high PQY of 27.2 %, and the phosphorescence luminance reach 500 lx at a delay of 0.1 s, which rendered them visible under a bright field. Furthermore, we explored the potential application of these CDs for anti-counterfeiting and crack detection in a bright field. Our work provides a facile strategy for realising highly efficient and strong emission phosphorescence in CD systems and is expected to promote the development of novel smart materials. Although the synergistic design of CEE and LBL has improved the loading capacity of CD materials, how to achieve better loading capacity and stability in more complex environments or different substrates is still a challenge. In the future, more efficient designs can be explored to further enhance the phosphorescence performance.

Jing Tan: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Daiqi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Investigation. Chunyi Lu: Resources, Investigation. Yi Li: Validation, Resources, Investigation. Xingyu Gu: Validation, Resources. Peng Sha: Validation, Supervision. Songnan Qu: Validation, Supervision. Xin Wang: Supervision. Lipei Fu: Validation, Supervision. Qijun Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52473281), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (BK20231537), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M732978) and Sponsored by CNPC Innovation Found (2024DQ02-0119). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Ning from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.162102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reversible and color-variable afterglow luminescence of carbon dots triggered by water for multi-level encryption and decryption
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Han, P. Li, Y. Deng, H. Li, Reversible and color-variable afterglow luminescence of carbon dots triggered by water for multi-level encryption and decryption, Chem. Eng. J. 415 (2021). doi: 10.1016/j.cej.2021.128999.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo‐stimulated polychromatic room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots
Small
